course.

Sounds great.
Well then, let's go ahead and dive into a little slides I have.
This was just a slide to introduce Phil if he wasn't here, but you got a chance to meet him.
Uh, so for today's session, we're just gonna start with the foundations then we'll talk about, looks like everybody's got logged in, so that's a great first step.

Uh, we'll then talk about what exactly is apartment IQ and what can it do for you, uh, how to leverage the tools to make Make, uh, data-informed decisions, where to access answers to common questions, and then who to contact if you need support.
So, just to kind of elaborate on what apartment IQ is and perhaps how it's different than other tools out there, apartment IQ is meant to be an automated market survey tool.
And so what we do is we look to landlord websites on a nightly basis and pull that information into reports that can be sorted and filtered.
Now the difference between apartment IQs, information, and perhaps some um other tools out there is that we're getting it from 100% public sources, and it's updated every 24 hours.
So there's really nothing for anybody to do in terms of data entry.

It also does not require uh competitors to be a part of our ecosystem in order for us to track them, because the information is pulled right from the website without anybody having to enter information.
Now we're tracking upwards of 3 million properties in the United States at this time.

Obviously, there could be a few properties that are missing here or there if they're brand new builds, or just simply have never been tracked before, but we're happy to get them added in as competitive properties if necessary.
Now I would love for today's conversation to be interactive, so I know a few of us have gotten into the system.
Please ask questions.
Um, you can interrupt me at any time, drop them in the chat, or you can leverage those reactions to let me know how you're feeling about the data today.

OK, so before we go ahead and dive into the content today, would love for somebody to let me know perhaps one of the properties that you'd like to analyze today, and then we can talk about all the reports and options within there.
Grow out of that you'd like to see.
How about um how about Amber Pines at Foster's Ridge in Texas.
Perfect.
OK.

So first and foremost, apartment IQ is going to be organized into several different reports.

The first here is the market survey, and it's gonna show you everything in regards to advertised occupancy, concessions, fees, amenities, pricing, and it's also where we can extract some reports and schedule reports as well.
We'll also talk about some of these other reports.
I'll do kind of a high level of these ones.
This will show you a performance of bed types, so it looks like we just have threes and fours at this property.
We'll also show you the floor plan information, um, if you have multiple floor plans at your property, we can dive into that data.
We can also see unit specific data.

So this is another thing that makes our tools stand out um in comparison to competitors, because we're not just looking at floor plan or bed type information, we're really getting down to the nitty gritty of individualized units, uh, so we're going to be able to dive into that and then also historicals, so we're able to actually access up to 4 years of history regarding your property and your comps, so we're able to see that tracked over time.
Now this data set is on a little bit of a leaner side.
Typically, we recommend that you track somewhere between 5 to 15 comps in a data set.
We may want to leverage a different property for our demo today just so we have a little bit more data to work with.
Is there another property that we might want to use?
We do Carma villas.
Hey, for this one, you probably only see 2 because I definitely edited and took some out, um,
yeah, so because they're multi-family, so the ones that we were when I was testing it, um, kind of edited the compet which is Why there was it.

So we have a couple of different user permissions within apartment IQ and if you have like an editor permission, which means you can change the competitive properties, you'll actually have a button in the top right hand corner that will allow you to see the comps that you're currently tracking, the comps that we're suggesting, and then also allow you to see all of the comps on a map view that you may want to explore.
So for Carmel villas, these are the current properties that the um are in the comp sets, but we can always change these and adjust them.
We can add in new properties or even explore additional um options over here, and if you find something of interest it's as easy as clicking add comp to add it to the set.
Now, a lot of people ask me how do we determine comp score, so I do want to uh just kind of break that down.
So apartment IQ leverages a math equation to compare competitive properties to the subject, and it's all the metrics that you're seeing to the right there in addition to story count.
So for BTR properties, we know that you're probably not gonna want to compare it to a high rise, so we're taking that into consideration when we're evaluating that comp score.
However, please know that if a property has a lower comp score, but you know that they are a competitor of that property, maybe they lose potential renterss to those properties all the time.
We still encourage you to include it.
The reason that comp scores are reduced is typically due to the fact that it's Farther away from the standard of the uh subject property.
So if it has a very significant difference in total units, it's gonna be a lower comp score.
If it's very far away or the year built is significantly different, it'll be a lower comp score.
So just keep that in mind uh when you're reviewing that here.
Now if you don't find a property that you're looking to add, it's really easy to search for it.
So we would just literally type in the address or the name.
But if you still don't find it, you can always request us to build it.

So the way that you would do that is to just provide us with the address and the website, and then as long as that website has live pricing with the unit pricing on it, or in this case the the property pricing for the BTR more than likely we can pull it in to apartment IQ, um, but we just have to have it publicly listed in order for us to track it.

OK, so let's talk about the metrics that you're seeing here.
So first and foremost, the metrics along the left can be customized.
So if you click customize metrics, each of you is gonna have your own unique login, and this is only gonna affect your view.
So choose what is important to you and deselect what isn't.
From there you would just hit apply and it's going to apply it to the customizations here.
If you oversee or want to see a lot of different properties, you can apply that to everything that you have access to, so that way you don't have to customize each and every one.
Now once that's set, we can now start filtering and sorting various metrics.

For example, if you're curious which property in a market has had the most applicants in a given week, you can sort this from high to low or low to high, and it's gonna position your property against your comps in the market.
Now we um capture applications as units that have been taken down offline.
So as soon as they are removed offline, we're gonna count it as an application, and it's gonna sit in that status for 7 days.
If the unit is relisted during that time, you'll see it relisted within the metrics here.
So here you can see that uh one unit that's available, but it was relisted within that time frame.
And then if it stays offline for 7 days, we're going to capture it as an official lease.
We assume that if a unit stays offline for a full 7 days, the unit has been leased, the renter has been approved.
Now from there, in addition to that, a couple other questions that commonly arise are around advertised occupancy.
So advertised occupancy is slightly different than perhaps uh physically reported on occupancy due to the fact that advertised occupancy is all based on what's advertised.

So what we do is we look to the property websites to see how many units are advertised, how many are vacant, and then we're able to determine an approximation or a projection of how occupied that property is.
Now, we are able to get around filtering, OK?
So I know.
A lot of properties will create like a scarcity model.
Maybe they have 21 beds available, right?
You're probably not gonna show every 21 of those one beds.
You might filter some out to push some of the ones that are vacant or some of the ones that are coming up for, um, vacancy a little sooner.
Um, we are able to see the units as long as they're listed on a website.
If they're not listed, perhaps marked as do not list, or it's a down unit under construction, or it's never been published to the website at all.
Perhaps at a lease up you might not have all of your units published yet.
Those are all things to take into consideration because those will not be counted towards this occupancy metric.
Do you have any questions on that?
Our team is currently working on some uh updates to the advertised occupancy metrics for lease up properties.

Any comments, feedback there?

We're gonna show you the most recent one, and then you can also see that unit mix over here to the right hand side.

Within all of these um units, you're actually going to be able to access the individualized unit price history, so you can see when it was listed and then it's still available for this version, but if it was applied to released you'd see what price was it removed from the website at um when you get to the end there.

We're gonna have a 30, 60, and 90 day trend report for you, showcasing performance metrics, pricing, total listings.

We're gonna show you bed count data.
Floor plan data.
